items.title: The Core Financial Statements
items.description: A complete set of general-purpose financial statements typically comprises a statement of financial position (balance sheet), a statement of profit or loss and other comprehensive income, a statement of changes in equity, a statement of cash flows, and accompanying notes. Together they portray an entity's financial position at a point in time and its performance and cash movements over a reporting period. Each statement is interconnected: profit flows into equity, and cash flow reconciles opening and closing cash balances.
items.applicability: Applies to any entity preparing periodic financial reports for owners, regulators, lenders, or tax authorities.
items.example: A year-end pack containing a balance sheet as at 31 December, an income statement for the 12 months then ended, an equity reconciliation, a cash flow statement, and explanatory notes.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Chart of Accounts
items.description: A chart of accounts is the structured list of every ledger account an organisation uses to classify financial transactions, usually organised by account type (assets, liabilities, equity, income, expenses). It provides a consistent coding scheme so that transactions are recorded uniformly and can be aggregated into financial statements. A well-designed chart balances granularity for management insight against simplicity for maintainability.
items.applicability: Foundational to any double-entry bookkeeping or ERP/general-ledger system.
items.example: Account 1000 Cash, 1200 Accounts Receivable, 2000 Accounts Payable, 3000 Share Capital, 4000 Sales Revenue, 5000 Cost of Sales.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: General Ledger
items.description: The general ledger is the central record that holds all of an entity's account balances and the postings that build them, organised according to the chart of accounts. Every journal entry ultimately lands in the ledger, where debits and credits accumulate per account. The ledger is the authoritative source from which a trial balance and the financial statements are derived.
items.applicability: Central to all accrual and cash-basis accounting systems and to any financial close process.
items.example: The Accounts Receivable ledger account shows an opening balance, all invoice debits and receipt credits during the month, and a closing balance carried forward.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Journal Entries and Double-Entry Bookkeeping
items.description: A journal entry records a financial transaction as balanced debits and credits, reflecting the double-entry principle that every transaction affects at least two accounts and total debits always equal total credits. This self-balancing structure provides an arithmetic integrity check across the ledger. Entries carry a date, narrative, and supporting reference so the transaction can be traced and audited.
items.applicability: Applies to every recorded financial transaction in a double-entry system, whether posted manually or by automated subledgers.
items.example: To record a EUR 1,000 cash sale: Debit Cash 1,000; Credit Sales Revenue 1,000.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Accrual Basis of Accounting
items.description: Under the accrual basis, transactions and events are recognised when they occur rather than when cash is received or paid, matching economic activity to the period in which it arises. Revenue is recorded when earned and expenses when incurred, giving a more faithful picture of performance than cash accounting. Accruals and prepayments are the mechanism that shifts cash-timing differences into the correct period.
items.applicability: Required by most general-purpose financial reporting frameworks for entities of any meaningful size.
items.example: Electricity consumed in December but invoiced in January is accrued as a December expense with a corresponding accrued liability.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Revenue Recognition Concepts
items.description: Modern revenue recognition follows a control-transfer model in which revenue is recognised when an entity satisfies a performance obligation by transferring a promised good or service to a customer. A common framework identifies the contract, separates distinct performance obligations, determines and allocates the transaction price, and recognises revenue as obligations are satisfied over time or at a point in time. The aim is to depict the transfer of goods or services at the amount the entity expects to be entitled to.
items.applicability: Applies to contracts with customers across most industries under IFRS-style frameworks.
items.example: A software vendor selling a 12-month subscription recognises the fee evenly over the service period rather than entirely at contract signing.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Expense Recognition and Matching
items.description: Expenses are recognised in the period in which the related economic benefits are consumed or the related revenues are earned, applying the matching concept. Costs directly tied to revenue are expensed when that revenue is recognised, while period costs are expensed as incurred. Where future benefit exists, expenditure is capitalised as an asset and charged to profit or loss over time.
items.applicability: Applies whenever costs must be allocated between the income statement and the balance sheet across reporting periods.
items.example: Cost of goods sold is recognised at the same time as the related sales revenue, while monthly office rent is expensed in the month it covers.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Assets, Liabilities and Equity
items.description: An asset is a resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected; a liability is a present obligation arising from past events expected to result in an outflow of resources; equity is the residual interest in the assets after deducting liabilities. These three elements form the accounting equation, where assets equal liabilities plus equity. Recognition depends on the item meeting its definition and being measurable reliably.
items.applicability: Foundational to the structure of the statement of financial position for all reporting entities.
items.example: A delivery van (asset) bought with a bank loan (liability) financed partly by owner contributions (equity) keeps the accounting equation in balance.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Asset Impairment Concepts
items.description: Impairment arises when the carrying amount of an asset exceeds the amount the entity can recover from using or selling it, requiring the asset to be written down to its recoverable amount. The recoverable amount is generally the higher of fair value less costs to sell and value in use, the latter based on discounted future cash flows. Impairment losses are charged to profit or loss, and some may be reversed if conditions improve, subject to standard-specific limits.
items.applicability: Applies to non-financial assets such as property, plant, equipment, intangibles, and goodwill that may lose value.
items.example: A production line carried at 500,000 whose recoverable amount falls to 350,000 after a market downturn is written down by a 150,000 impairment charge.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Expected Credit Loss Concept
items.description: The expected credit loss (ECL) concept requires entities to recognise a forward-looking allowance for potential losses on financial assets rather than waiting for a loss to be incurred. ECL is a probability-weighted estimate of credit losses over the life or near-term horizon of an exposure, reflecting the time value of money and reasonable, supportable information about past, current, and forecast conditions. It shifts provisioning from an incurred-loss to an anticipated-loss footing.
items.applicability: Relevant to lenders, banks, and any entity holding receivables, loans, or debt instruments measured under an ECL framework.
items.example: A bank books a small loss allowance on a newly issued, performing loan to reflect the statistical chance of default over the next twelve months.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: IFRS-Style Reporting Principles
items.description: IFRS-style frameworks are built on a conceptual foundation emphasising faithful representation, relevance, comparability, verifiability, timeliness, and understandability, applied on a going-concern and accrual basis. They favour a principles-based approach in which substance prevails over legal form and disclosure fills gaps where measurement is uncertain. The objective is to give existing and potential investors, lenders, and creditors information useful for economic decisions.
items.applicability: Applies to entities reporting under or converging with International Financial Reporting Standards.
items.example: An entity classifies a sale-and-leaseback by its economic substance rather than its contractual label when the substance differs from the form.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: IFRS 9-Style Credit Impairment Concepts
items.description: An IFRS 9-style impairment model classifies financial assets into stages reflecting changes in credit risk since initial recognition, driving whether a twelve-month or lifetime expected credit loss is recognised. Assets that have not significantly deteriorated attract a twelve-month allowance, those with a significant increase in credit risk move to lifetime ECL, and credit-impaired assets recognise lifetime losses with interest computed on the net carrying amount. The staging mechanism makes provisioning responsive to deterioration before default occurs.
items.applicability: Relevant to financial institutions and entities measuring loan and receivable portfolios under an IFRS 9-aligned framework.
items.example: A loan that breaches an early-warning threshold migrates from Stage 1 (twelve-month ECL) to Stage 2 (lifetime ECL), increasing its allowance.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Audit Trail
items.description: An audit trail is the chronological, traceable record that links each reported figure back to its source transactions, supporting documents, and the users or systems that created or modified them. It enables an independent party to follow a balance from the financial statements down to individual postings and original evidence. A robust audit trail underpins the reliability, accountability, and reproducibility of financial information.
items.applicability: Required for internal control, external audit, and regulatory examination across all accounting environments.
items.example: A trade balance traces to a journal posting, which references an invoice number, a goods-received note, and the approving user with a timestamp.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Account Reconciliation
items.description: Reconciliation is the control process of comparing two independent records of the same balance and explaining or correcting any differences between them. It confirms that ledger balances agree with external evidence such as bank statements, subledgers, or counterparty confirmations, and that timing or error differences are identified. Performed regularly, reconciliation is a primary defence against misstatement, fraud, and unrecorded transactions.
items.applicability: Applies to cash, intercompany, receivable, payable, and control accounts during routine close and review cycles.
items.example: A bank reconciliation matches the ledger cash balance to the bank statement, listing outstanding cheques and deposits in transit as reconciling items.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Period Close Process
items.description: The period close is the structured sequence of tasks that finalises the ledger for a reporting period, including posting accruals, running reconciliations, reviewing exceptions, and locking the period against further changes. It ensures all transactions of the period are captured and that opening balances roll forward cleanly. A disciplined close shortens reporting timelines and reduces the risk of late adjustments and restatement.
items.applicability: Applies to monthly, quarterly, and annual financial reporting cycles in any accounting organisation.
items.example: A month-end checklist covers cut-off testing, accrual posting, reconciliation sign-off, management review, and period locking before reporting.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Financial Controls
items.description: Financial controls are the policies, procedures, and system safeguards that protect the accuracy, completeness, and integrity of financial information and assets. They include segregation of duties, authorisation limits, automated validations, and independent review, and operate as preventive or detective measures. Effective controls reduce the likelihood and impact of error and fraud and provide assurance to management, auditors, and regulators.
items.applicability: Applies across the financial reporting lifecycle in regulated and unregulated entities alike.
items.example: Requiring a second approver for payments above a defined threshold enforces segregation of duties and limits unilateral disbursement.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Management Accounts
items.description: Management accounts are internal financial reports prepared to support decision-making, planning, and control, rather than for external statutory filing. They are typically produced frequently and may include budgets versus actuals, segment profitability, key performance indicators, and forward forecasts tailored to management's needs. Because they are internal, their format is flexible and can incorporate non-statutory measures.
items.applicability: Used by management teams, boards, and budget holders to steer operations between statutory reporting dates.
items.example: A monthly management pack shows departmental margin against budget, a rolling cash forecast, and commentary on key variances.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Statutory Reporting
items.description: Statutory reporting is the preparation and filing of financial statements and returns required by law or regulation, in a prescribed format and to defined deadlines. It must comply with the applicable financial reporting framework and any jurisdiction-specific company or tax law obligations. Statutory filings are often public, subject to audit, and carry legal consequences for non-compliance or misstatement.
items.applicability: Applies to incorporated entities and regulated businesses with legal filing obligations to registrars, regulators, or tax authorities.
items.example: A company files its audited annual financial statements and an associated tax return with the national registrar and revenue authority by the statutory deadline.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Consolidation Basics
items.description: Consolidation combines the financial statements of a parent and the entities it controls into a single set of group accounts presented as if they were one economic entity. The process aggregates assets, liabilities, income, and expenses, eliminates intragroup transactions and balances, and recognises any non-controlling interest in the equity and results of subsidiaries. Goodwill or a bargain-purchase gain may arise on acquisition where the price differs from the fair value of net assets acquired.
items.applicability: Applies to parent entities that control one or more subsidiaries and must present group financial statements.
items.example: Intragroup sales between a parent and its subsidiary are eliminated so that consolidated revenue reflects only sales to external customers.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Accounting Estimates
items.description: Accounting estimates are monetary amounts in financial statements that cannot be measured precisely and must instead be derived using judgment, assumptions, and available data. Examples include useful lives of assets, provisions, fair values, and expected credit losses, all of which carry estimation uncertainty. Estimates are revised prospectively as new information emerges, and the basis and sensitivity of significant estimates are typically disclosed.
items.applicability: Applies wherever measurement depends on assumptions about uncertain future outcomes.
items.example: Management estimates the useful life of new machinery at eight years to compute annual depreciation, revising it if usage patterns change.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Materiality
items.description: Information is material if omitting, misstating, or obscuring it could reasonably be expected to influence the decisions of users of the financial statements. Materiality is assessed in terms of size and nature, and is entity-specific rather than a fixed threshold. It guides judgments about recognition, measurement, presentation, and the level of disclosure needed without overwhelming users with immaterial detail.
items.applicability: Applies to preparation and audit decisions about what to record, correct, and disclose.
items.example: A minor rounding difference of a few units may be deemed immaterial, while a small but fraudulent misstatement could be material due to its nature.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Data Lineage in Financial Reporting
items.description: Data lineage describes the documented path that financial data follows from its origin through every transformation, aggregation, and system until it appears in a report. In financial reporting it makes balances explainable and reproducible, showing exactly which source records and calculation rules produced a figure. Strong lineage supports control, regulatory traceability, and rapid root-cause analysis when numbers are questioned.
items.applicability: Relevant to automated reporting pipelines, data warehouses, and regulatory reporting where figures must be traced end to end.
items.example: A regulatory exposure figure can be traced from the report cell back through aggregation logic to the source loan-level records that fed it.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.

items.title: Trial Balance
items.description: A trial balance is a listing of all ledger account balances at a point in time, with total debits and total credits expected to be equal under double-entry bookkeeping. It serves as an arithmetic check on posting integrity and as the starting point for preparing financial statements. While a balanced trial balance confirms equal debits and credits, it does not by itself guarantee the absence of misclassification or omission errors.
items.applicability: Used at period close and during preparation of financial statements in double-entry systems.
items.example: An unadjusted trial balance is extracted at month-end, adjusting entries are posted, and an adjusted trial balance feeds the income statement and balance sheet.
items.limitation: Educational/professional summary; does not replace the licensed accounting standards, professional accountant judgment, or statutory advice.
